fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need a small utility library for the pooling system we've been building. Basically it needs to handle a few things: expressing time-based wait periods in a way that doesn't break when different parts of the code use different units (you know how that's been causing those subtle comparison bugs), some logic for deciding when a pooled resource is 'too old' to reuse, and a config object for the pool itself. For the age stuff, we need at least two flavors — one simple cutoff, and one that spreads retirements across a window (similar to how we handled the jitter logic in that old connection module). There should also be a way to stack two of those age rules together so both can be enforced at once. The config thing is pretty straightforward, just needs a size and two on/off switches — check the existing defaults we agreed on internally, I don't have them in front of me. One thing that keeps biting us: bad inputs (like missing units or out-of-range thresholds) are currently bubbling up as raw exceptions which looks terrible in logs. We need those to come out as clean, categorized error messages instead. The boundary behavior on the age checks has also been inconsistent — make sure it's pinned down precisely so we stop getting off-by-one complaints from the team.

trigger_keywords: what is the base unit for time conversion
which unit does duration convert to
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system's base unit is always NANOSECONDS. All duration values must be convertible to nanoseconds as the common denominator for comparison and storage. For example, 13 MILLISECONDS becomes base_value=13000000 in nanoseconds.

trigger_keywords: are zero or negative durations allowed
what happens with a zero wait duration
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Zero and negative magnitudes are explicitly accepted for durations — they conventionally mean 'no waiting at all'. They must be round-tripped faithfully with no conversion artifact. There is no lower-bound validation on the duration magnitude itself.

trigger_keywords: what happens when unit is null
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the time unit is null/absent, construction must be rejected with a two-line neutral error: 'error=null_argument' on the first line and 'param=unit' on the second line. This applies to duration creation, max_age policy, and spread policy alike. No host-language exception identity should be exposed.

trigger_keywords: equality check for durations in different units
are two durations equal if same absolute length
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two durations are equal if and only if they represent the same absolute span of time when both are converted to the base unit (nanoseconds). The units they were expressed in are irrelevant. Equal durations must also produce the same hash value. Durations of different absolute lengths are not equal and must not share a hash.

trigger_keywords: is age equal to threshold considered expired
strict greater than or greater than or equal for expiry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The max_age policy uses strict-greater-than semantics: an item whose age is at or below the threshold is still valid (expired=false). Only an item strictly older than the threshold is expired (expired=true). Age == threshold means NOT expired.

trigger_keywords: what threshold is rejected as illegal
max age must be at least how much
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The max_age threshold magnitude must be at least 1. A magnitude below 1 (i.e., 0 or negative) is rejected with 'error=illegal_argument' on the first line and 'param=max_age' on the second line. Validation of the threshold is performed before checking the unit.

trigger_keywords: when is item expired in windowed policy
lower and upper bound behavior for spread
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the spread/windowed policy: an item strictly younger than the lower bound is never expired (expired=false). An item whose age is at or beyond the upper bound is always expired (expired=true). Between the bounds the verdict is indeterminate. The lower bound uses strict-less-than; the upper bound uses greater-than-or-equal.

trigger_keywords: lower bound minimum value for spread
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the spread policy: the lower magnitude must be at least 1, otherwise 'error=illegal_argument' / 'param=lower'. The upper magnitude must be strictly greater than the lower magnitude, otherwise 'error=illegal_argument' / 'param=upper'. The unit null check yields 'error=null_argument' / 'param=unit'. Validation order is: lower, then upper, then unit.

The composite policy applies OR semantics: the combined result is expired=true if either the first or second underlying policy is expired. The combined result is expired=false only when both underlying policies report fresh. The output echoes both underlying verdicts (first=, second=) and then the combined expired= result.

trigger_keywords: what are the defaults for the config holder
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A freshly initialized pool configuration holder has: size=10, precise_leak_detection=true, background_expiration=false. These are the fixed defaults. Any setter operation changes only the targeted field; the others retain their defaults.

trigger_keywords: what setters exist on pool config
how to change pool size or toggles
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The pool configuration supports four operations via 'op': 'defaults' reads the freshly-initialized state; 'set_size' sets the integer target size; 'set_precise_leak_detection' sets the precise-leak-detection boolean; 'set_background_expiration' sets the background-expiration boolean. After any operation, the full snapshot (size, precise_leak_detection, background_expiration) is reported.

trigger_keywords: error message format for bad inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All validation errors must be rendered as exactly two neutral category lines with no exception class names or stack traces: first line is 'error=<category>' (either 'error=null_argument' or 'error=illegal_argument') and second line is 'param=<parameter_name>'. Each line ends with a newline. No host-language runtime exception identity may appear in output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'duration', 'duration_compare', 'max_age', 'spread', 'composite', 'config', 'defaults', 'set_size'. Each value maps to one feature.

give me the feature1_duration_value test file
what does feature1 duration value expect

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the jitter logic in that old connection module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The spread/windowed validity policy (Feature 4) with lower and upper age bounds, where retirements are distributed across a window rather than at a single cutoff instant — implemented in the spread action handler using lower_millis and upper_millis

context_pointers.vague_reference: check the existing defaults we agreed on internally
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Pool configuration defaults defined in Feature 6: size=10, precise_leak_detection=true, background_expiration=false

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more details on that specific point. Please check the existing codebase patterns and the test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise boundary behavior — the test cases are the authoritative contract for edge cases I haven't documented here.

feature1_duration_value.json.description: Describe a duration value built from a numeric magnitude and a named time unit. The duration reports back its original magnitude and unit unchanged, plus the magnitude re-expressed in the system's finest base unit (nanoseconds) via an exact unit conversion. The magnitude may be zero or negative (meaning no waiting), which is accepted. If the time unit is absent (null), construction is rejected with a neutral null-argument error naming the unit parameter.

feature2_duration_equivalence.json.description: Compare two duration values for equivalence and hash consistency. Two durations are equivalent when they represent the same absolute span of time after converting both to the common base unit, regardless of the units they were originally expressed in; equivalent durations must also share the same hash. Durations representing different absolute spans are not equivalent. Each input supplies two durations, each as a magnitude plus a named time unit, and the result reports whether they are equivalent and whether their hashes agree.

feature3_max_age_validity.json.description: A single-threshold age validity policy: a pooled item is invalid once its age strictly exceeds a maximum permitted age. The policy is built from a maximum age magnitude plus a named time unit, and the maximum age is reported in milliseconds. Given an item's age in milliseconds, the policy reports whether the item has expired: ages at or below the threshold are valid (not expired), ages strictly above it are expired. The threshold magnitude must be at least one, otherwise construction is rejected with a neutral illegal-argument error naming the max-age parameter; if the time unit is absent (null), construction is rejected with a neutral null-argument error naming the unit parameter.

feature4_spread_validity.json.description: A windowed age validity policy defined by a lower and an upper age bound. Items younger than the lower bound are never expired; items at or beyond the upper bound are always expired; between the two bounds the verdict varies. The policy is built from a lower magnitude, an upper magnitude and a named time unit, and both bounds are reported in milliseconds. Given an item's age in milliseconds, the policy reports whether the item has expired for the deterministic regions (below the lower bound, and at or above the upper bound). The lower bound must be at least one and the upper bound must be strictly greater than the lower bound, otherwise construction is rejected with a neutral illegal-argument error naming the offending bound; if the time unit is absent (null), construction is rejected with a neutral null-argument error naming the unit parameter.

feature5_composite_validity.json.description: A composite validity policy that combines two underlying policies with OR semantics: an item is considered expired if either of the two underlying policies considers it expired, and only valid when both consider it valid. Each input fixes the verdict of the first and second underlying policy (each either expired or fresh), and the result reports both underlying verdicts together with the combined outcome.

feature6_pool_configuration.json.description: The pool configuration holder exposes a target pool size plus two boolean toggles: precise leak detection (enabled by default) and background expiration (disabled by default). Each input names an operation: either reading the freshly-initialized defaults, or applying a single setter (set the size, toggle precise leak detection, or toggle background expiration). The result reports the full configuration snapshot after the operation, so that the targeted field reflects the change while the untouched fields retain their default values.
